name: New Fortress Energy Inc.

profile.description: New Fortress Energy Inc. is an integrated gas-to-power energy infrastructure company providing LNG terminals, power plants, and logistics across the Caribbean, Latin America, and beyond. The company is undergoing a major debt restructuring via a UK Restructuring Plan, reducing corporate debt from $5.7B to $527.5M by splitting into BrazilCo (private, held by creditors) and New NFE (publicly traded). Founder and CEO Wesley Edens owns 27.66% of shares. Founded in 1998, headquartered in New York.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): NFE pioneered the Fast LNG concept and built small-scale LNG terminal infrastructure in markets previously inaccessible to natural gas — a genuine innovation in energy access that opened Caribbean and Latin American markets to cleaner fuel [1]. However, the Fast LNG initiative has become the company's undoing, with project delays, cost overruns, and aggressive financing undermining the original vision [2]. No successful new product launch or business transformation in the last 2 years. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI deployment, products, or quantified AI-driven savings — the company is in financial restructuring mode with no technology innovation evident [1]. Market Share (1/2): NFE established a pioneering position in small-scale LNG infrastructure for island and emerging market nations, though revenue declined 51.9% to $226.9M in Q1 2026 as assets are being carved out in the restructuring [1]. Product Pipeline (0/2): No new projects announced; the restructuring splits existing assets between BrazilCo (creditor-owned) and New NFE, effectively shrinking the company's asset base rather than expanding it [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): No meaningful R&D spending evident for a capital-intensive infrastructure company in financial distress.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): NFE's stated purpose — 'reducing carbon emissions and fostering economic development through universal access to cleaner, affordable, and reliable energy' — is genuinely impactful and service-oriented, connecting LNG infrastructure to economic development in underserved markets [3]. Wesley Edens articulates this vision compellingly. However, the financial collapse and restructuring undermine the credibility of execution against this mission. Community Impact (1/2): NFE's LNG projects have measurably displaced diesel and heavy fuel oil in Caribbean nations, providing cleaner and more affordable power to communities — real, tangible impact in places like Puerto Rico and Jamaica [3]. Employee Retention (0/2): The company has reduced headcount from ~1,200 to 699, and the ongoing restructuring, going-concern warnings, and massive share dilution (existing shareholders diluted from 100% to 35%) create an environment of extreme uncertainty [1]. Insider Buying (0/2): No insider buying identified. CEO Edens' 27.66% stake ($174M) is being diluted to ~9.7% as creditors take control — net insider dilution, not conviction buying [2].

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Q1 2026 revenue fell 51.9% to $226.9M, net loss widened 128% to $399.9M ($1.40/share loss), and the company has triggered a UK Restructuring Plan to reduce $5.7B debt to $527.5M [1]. D/E is astronomically high — $8.57B debt against $182.6M equity yields ~4,691%, scoring 0.0. ROE of -221.5% scores 0.0 (2x weight). Revenue growth YoY of -50.8% is far below the -20% floor, scoring 0.0. Revenue growth 5yr CAGR of 2.9% scores 0.527 (2x weight). Forward P/E of 8.85 scores 1.0. Profit margin of -164.2% scores 0.0. Forward PEG of 22.35 scores 0.0 (2x weight). Weighted sum: 2.054/10 = 2.1, rounded to 2. Liquidity check: 12.16M volume x $0.62 = $7.5M, above $1M floor [4].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): NFE's core business — LNG terminals, regasification units, power plants, and shipping infrastructure — is entirely physical and structurally immune to AI disruption [1]. LNG liquefaction, maritime logistics, and power generation require capital-intensive physical assets that AI cannot substitute. The growing demand for electricity from AI data centers is structurally positive for natural gas-powered generation, potentially increasing demand for NFE's LNG-to-power infrastructure if the company survives its restructuring. Regulatory compliance, environmental permitting, and infrastructure operations create permanent moats against AI displacement. AI Opportunity (0/1): NFE generates no revenue from AI products or services and has no AI-related initiatives — the company is focused entirely on its debt restructuring and operational survival [1].

infinite_game.summary: NFE's financial collapse — $5.7B in debt requiring restructuring, existing shareholders diluted from 100% to 35%, net loss of $399.9M in a single quarter — is the terminal expression of a finite-game strategy. Wesley Edens' aggressive debt-funded expansion prioritized speed and scale over financial sustainability. Sinek would identify NFE as a company that confused ambition with courage, leveraging the future to finance the present until the debt structure collapsed under its own weight.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): NFE's purpose — universal access to cleaner, affordable energy — is genuinely noble and validated by real impact in Caribbean and Latin American communities where LNG displaced expensive, polluting diesel [3]. The Just Cause is real; the execution failure is financial, not purposive. However, a Just Cause that leads to near-bankruptcy through reckless financial engineering suggests the purpose was subordinated to growth ambition. Edens' dual role as CEO and major shareholder (27.66%) conflated personal wealth creation with the stated mission.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): The company's going-concern warnings, debt restructuring, headcount reduction from ~1,200 to 699, and impending dilution of existing equity by 65% create an environment of extreme uncertainty [1][2]. Employees face the prospect of their employer being split into two entities (BrazilCo and New NFE) with different ownership structures. This is a crisis environment incompatible with Sinek's Circle of Safety.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): NFE operated with a dominant competitive mindset — Edens' dealmaking approach prioritized speed and aggressive financing to preempt competitors in small-scale LNG markets rather than learning from rivals' strengths [2]. The 'Fast LNG' concept was about winning first-mover advantage, not about improving through competitive dynamics. The company's language focused on market capture rather than self-improvement.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (0/2): The UK Restructuring Plan splitting NFE into BrazilCo and New NFE is a reactive response to financial distress, not a proactive strategic reinvention in service of the Just Cause [1]. The company is being restructured by creditors, not by leadership following a vision. Asset carve-outs under duress are survival mechanisms, not existential flexibility.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): Edens' aggressive expansion — accumulating $5.7B in debt to build LNG infrastructure across multiple continents simultaneously — was reckless leverage masquerading as courage [2]. Courageous leadership invests in long-term capability while maintaining financial sustainability. NFE prioritized speed over solvency, resulting in a missed $163.8M interest payment, going-concern warnings, and a restructuring that dilutes existing shareholders by 65%. This is the opposite of Sinek's courage to lead.

investment_thesis: NFE's investment case rests entirely on the restructuring outcome. The UK Restructuring Plan reduces corporate debt from $5.7B to $527.5M and splits the company into BrazilCo (creditor-owned Brazilian assets) and New NFE (publicly traded LNG-to-power company) [1]. If the restructuring closes successfully by Q3 2026, existing shareholders retain 35% of a significantly deleveraged entity. The remaining LNG terminal and power generation assets have real operational value, and growing AI data center electricity demand creates a structural tailwind for natural gas power. Wesley Edens' 27.66% pre-dilution stake (retaining ~9.7% post-restructuring) provides aligned incentives.
thesis_continued: The bear case is overwhelming. Revenue declined 51.9% to $226.9M in Q1 2026, net loss widened to $399.9M, and the company's going-concern warning remains in effect [1]. Existing shareholders are being diluted from 100% to 35% as creditors exchange $5.7B in debt for equity, preferred stock, and new debt. At $0.62/share ($177M market cap), the stock has already declined ~95% from its 2024 highs. The restructuring faces execution risk — UK court approval, regulatory clearances, and creditor consent are all required by Q3 2026. Edens' track record includes 'project delays and aggressive financial strategy' that undermined operational execution [2]. Even in the optimistic scenario, the post-restructuring New NFE will be a smaller company with fewer assets and diluted ownership.
key_catalysts: Successful completion of the UK Restructuring Plan by Q3 2026, reducing debt from $5.7B to $527.5M and creating a viable New NFE entity with existing shareholders retaining 35% ownership
Operational stabilization of remaining LNG-to-power assets post-restructuring, with growing AI-driven electricity demand creating structural tailwind for natural gas generation
Natural gas price environment and LNG demand growth supporting cash flow recovery in the deleveraged New NFE entity
key_risks: Restructuring failure — if the UK Restructuring Plan does not receive court approval or creditor consent by Q3 2026, the company could face Chapter 11 bankruptcy where equity holders typically receive nothing
Existing shareholder dilution from 100% to 35% is already locked in; any further restructuring complications or asset impairments could reduce equity value to zero
Revenue decline of 51.9% YoY and net loss of $399.9M in Q1 2026 demonstrate ongoing operational deterioration — the company may not generate positive cash flow before the restructuring closes
